title: Shea butter hair mask
subtitle: A rich anhydrous hair mask with shea, coconut, and argan for dry or damaged hair
excerpt: This is a deeply conditioning anhydrous hair mask made from three fats: shea butter for softening, coconut oil for penetration, and argan oil for shine. It applies to dry hair, sits for 30 minutes, and washes out cleanly. No preservative is required as there is no water in the formula.
sourceNotes: Anhydrous hair mask formulation based on natural cosmetics formulation references. Shea butter and coconut oil hair penetration studies from International Journal of Trichology.

recipe.yieldDescription: 100 g hair mask (fills a 100 ml jar)
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the recipe and store in separate jars.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use once set. No cure time required.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt shea and coconut oil gently. Remove from heat before adding argan oil and essential oil.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months. Discard if the mask smells rancid or the texture changes significantly. Keep water out of the jar during use.

glossaryTerms.definition: Containing no water. Anhydrous products do not require a preservative and are stable at room temperature as long as water is not introduced into the jar during use.
A fat extracted from the nut of the African shea tree. Rich in oleic and stearic fatty acids, it is deeply conditioning and melts readily on contact with skin and hair at body temperature.
A substance that forms a barrier on the hair shaft or skin surface to reduce moisture loss. Shea butter and coconut oil are both occlusive as well as conditioning.
The percentage of essential oil in a finished leave-on product. For a hair mask used on the scalp, 0.5% is the conservative recommended maximum.

content.text: hair mask uses three fats at different saturation levels.
(60%) provides the body; coconut oil (30%) penetrates the hair shaft more deeply than most oils; argan oil (10%) adds shine and seals the cuticle. The
nature of shea and coconut means the mask holds moisture in the hair shaft during the treatment period. Lavender essential oil is included at a 0.5%
for fragrance and scalp comfort. Shelf life is 12 months.

Melt the shea butter and coconut oil together in a small heatproof bowl over a double boiler or in a microwave in 20-second intervals until just liquid. Remove from heat. Add the argan oil and stir to combine. Allow to cool to room temperature. Add the lavender essential oil once the mixture is below 40 degrees Celsius. Pour into the jar and leave to set at room temperature for approximately 1 hour.

To use: take a small amount (walnut-sized for short hair, larger for long) and work between the palms to melt it. Apply to dry hair from mid-lengths to ends, avoiding the scalp if hair is prone to oiliness. For a deep treatment, apply to the scalp and all hair. Leave for at least 30 minutes, then wash out thoroughly with shampoo.

Shelf life is 12 months stored in a cool, dry place. Use a clean spatula or spoon to scoop the mask from the jar, not wet fingers, to prevent introducing water and shortening the shelf life. Discard if the mask smells rancid or develops an unpleasant odour.
